Paper.li Friday, 8 March 2013 Biotech 8 Review Comedy is all about trying to figure out how far you can go in my opinion. When people go to see a stand-up comic, what the audience is often looking for is someone with a great sense of timing and a feel for the crowd. They may not say it specifically, but when a comic knows how to handle the people they are talking to then modifying what they are doing to fit. A few extra words or a change in the type of words being said can make all difference between an okay performance and one that gets everyone on their feet. "Biotech 8," while not strictly fitting into traditional comedy, plays with similar boundaries by diving deep into themes often reserved for documentaries: genetic engineering. The film utilizes humor to explore the sometimes absurd possibilities of biotechnology advancements—a blend unique enough in itself—but does it manage this dance without missing a step? At its core "Biotech 8" is an exploration masked as entertainment that seeks not just laughs, but engagement with some weighty topics—cloning ethics being one. The film doesn't shy away from asking hard questions or presenting speculative yet scientifically plausible scenarios involving enhanced humans and modified animals. The comedic elements primarily come in through the exaggerated characters; scientists turned mad geniuses offer humorous takes on their creations' unintended side effects, inviting both laughter at these fictional follies as well thought-provoking reflection. The humor is often situational but relies heavily upon delivering just enough science to be believable yet overstated for comic effect. In terms of technical execution and acting perf

texts.full_text: Letter from the Commissioner Dear Traveler, Welcome to the U.S. Virgin Islands! Our trio of islands - St. Croix, St. John, and St. Thomas - each possess their own unique charm and appeal, inviting you to explore and experience their distinct offerings. St. Thomas, our most cosmopolitan island, is a vibrant hub of activity. As the most visited port in thE Caribbean, Charlotte Amalie, our capital, offers a stunning harbor, world-class dining, an exciting nightlife, and duty-free shopping galore. Soak up the sun on our beautiful beaches like Magens Bay or Coki Point, shop along the bustling waterfront, or visit iconic attractions such as Blackbeard's Castle and the 99 Steps. St. John, our smallest island, is a nature lover's paradise. With two-thirds of its land designated as a National Park, you'll find unspoiled beaches, crystal-clear waters perfect for snorkeling, and lush hiking trails waiting to be explored. Relax on the pristine sands of Trunk Bay or Honeymoon Beach, and don't miss out on the opportunity to camp under the stars in this tranquil haven. St. Croix, our westernmost island, is a melting pot of history, culture, and natural beauty. Here you'll find historic sites like Buck Island Reef National Monument and the Cruzan Rum Distillery, as well as stunning beaches such as Salt River Bay and Sandy Point. Experience the laid-back charm of Frederiksted town or Christiansted, our two vibrant yet relaxed ports. Each island offers a unique experience, so I encourage you to explore them all during your visit. Whether you're seeking adventure, relaxation, history, or culture, the U.S. Virgin Islands has something special in store for you. Welcome once again, and enjoy your time in our beautiful islands! Sincerely, [Your Name] Commissioner of T

texts.full_text: right words, with understanding what I want to say, with understanding how to say it. I struggle with expressing myself, with making myself understood, with making myself heard. But reading helps. reading helps me to understand how other people communicate, how they express themselves, how they make themselves understood. Reading helps me to understand the nuances of language, the subtleties of communication, the art of articulation. Reading also helps me to understand myself better. It helps me to understand my own thoughts, my own feelings, my own experiences. It helps me to understand my autism, my own unique way of processing information, my own unique way of understanding the world. And that’s why I’ve come to realise that nicheless blogs are better for me, especially as an autistic person. A nicheless blog is a blog that doesn’t focus on a specific topic or theme. Instead, it covers a wide range of subjects, from personal experiences to current events, from book reviews to opinion pieces. It’s a blog that allows the writer to explore a variety of interests, to express a variety of thoughts, to share a variety of experiences. For me, a nicheless blog is better because it allows me to write about what I’m passionate about, what I’m interested in, what I’m thinking about. Session session = driver.session(); String queryResult = session.run("RETURN 'Hello, World!'") .single() .get(0) .asString(); assertEquals(queryResult , "Hello, World!"); } } } ``` ### Explanation - HTTP Connector: The HTTP connector is configured to listen on `localhost:7475`. This can be used for accessing the Neo4j Browser or other tools that use REST API. - Bolt Protocol: Bolt protocol listens on port 7689, which you would typically connect your application using a driver like Java's GraphDatabase.driver. - Authentication Disabled (`auth.auth_enabled=false`): This allows connections without needing to provide credentials. texts.full_text: ether the prefix "di-" is more Latin than the prefix "bi-", when indicating two-ness? (2) Are there published scholarly references on precisely this question, comparing the two prefixes? (3) Are there notable Latin words which simultaneously contain "bi" and "di"? Remarks. In a vague sense that I have no time or experience enough to substantiate, this seems to be the case, "di-" seeming significantly more Latin-ish than "bi-" which seems more Greek-ish. EDIT: like kindly pointed out by the responders, here this attribution of bi to Greek and di to Latin was an accidental mix-up. It is the other way round: bi is Latin, di is Greek. Of course, the difference is not clear-cut, there is organic growth and randomness involved, and e.g. in Greek both "bi-" and "di-" appear in composites. Both "bi-" and "di-" appear in countless scientific terms. A: di- is Greek and bi- is Latin The Proto-Indo-European root for "two" is reconstructed as *dwóh₁, from which the Greek prefix "di-" (δί-) is derived, meaning "twice" or "two". On the other hand, the Latin prefix "bi-" comes from the same root, but through a different development, and means "twice" or "two" as well. In Latin, "bi-" is indeed used to

texts.full_text: mEasuring the motivational aspects of cocaine reinforcement. In the procedure established here, each infusion of cocaine (0.25 mg/infusion) was initially made contingent on a lever press and was paired with a 20-s light conditioned stimulus (CS). When rats acquired stable rates of cocaine self-administration, the response requirement for cocaine was increased progressively to a second-order schedule of the type FI15 min(FR10:S), whereby the IV cocaine infusion was self-administered following the completion of the first FR10 responses (and CS presentation) after a 15-min fixed interval (FI) had elapsed. Evaluation of the animals' responding during the first, drug-free interval of each daily session provided a measure of cocaine-seeking behaviour, independent of other pharmacological effects of cocaine. This measure, referred to as "anticipatory responding," reflects the motivational state of the animal and the incentive value of the cocaine-paired CS. In this study, the effects of continuous cocaine access and the presence of the CS on the acquisition, maintenance, and reinstatement of cocaine self-administration were systematically investigated. Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNA) have recently emerged as important regulators in cancer cell proliferation and metastasis. However, the role of lncRNAs in metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) remains unclear. Here, single-cell RNA sequencing data were analyzed from primary renal cell carcinoma and paired metastatic renal cell carcinoma specimens, and characterized the expression profiles of over 10,000 genes, including 1,874 lncRNAs. Further analysis revealed that lncRNAs exhibit cancer type- and tissue-specific expression across ccRCC cells. texts.full_text: as aseptate filaments. The effect was reversed on removal of the antibiotic. The composition of walls isolaBenyzylpenicillin-induced filamentation or fibrillogenesis (fiber formation) refers to an unusual morphological change exhibited by some bacterial species, including Clostridium perfringens, in response to specific stress conditions such as antibiotic exposure. This phenomenon is not a standard growth characteristic but rather a reactive transformation displayed under certain adverse environmental pressures. In the case of Clostridium perfringens exposed to low concentrations of benzylpenicillin (also known as penicillin G), an inhibition occurs on normal cell division processes that involve septum formation and subsequent cytokinesis. These steps are critical for bacterial cell growth, where new cells split off from a parent bacterium. The antibiotic benzylpenicillin is part of the beta-lactam family, known to disrupt peptidoglycan synthesis which is fundamental in maintaining proper structural integrity within bacterial cells. Peptidoglycan acts as an essential component for both cell wall formation and stability—a lack or alteration can impair this function. When benzylpenicillin inhibits peptidoglycan cross-linking due to its structural similarity to trans-peptidase substrates, the bacteria lose their ability to perform regular septum division. Instead of dying off rapidly as one might expect under such conditions, C. perfringens cells react by initiating filamentation—essentially growing longer and thinner structures instead of short, round forms. This aseptate filament formation can be visualized microscopically, presenting an alternative mode for the bacteria to propagate when faced with peptidoglycan synthesis inhibition. These filaments are formed as chains or clusters without the separation observed in normal binary fission. However, once benzylpenicillin is removed from the growth medium (typical experiment practice includes either a wash step after exposure or removing the antibiotic at an appropriate time to analyze effects), conditions that previously caused inhibition may be restored. The effect of reduced division due to penicillin G dissipates and normal cell cycle progression resumes. In this case, American General and the Bank providedevidence that Ferguson had a history of back problems prior to her fall at City National Bank. They argued that the fall exacerbated these existing conditions, thus making it a second injury eligible for reimbursement from the SIF. However, the Louisiana Worker's Compensation Second Injury Board (Board) denied their request for reimbursement, findinginsufficient evidence to establish that Ferguson'scurrent condition was directly caused by or materially contributed toby her pre-existingwork-related back injuries. The Board concluded that while Ferguson may have had prior back issues, there was no conclusive proof linking those conditions to the fall and subsequent injury she sustained on October 24, 1986. American General and City National Bank appealed this decision, contending that the Board erred in itsinterpretation of the evidence. They arguedthat the preponderance of the evidencesupported their claim for reimbursement from the SIF. The Court of Appeal reviewed the record and found no manifest error in the Board's decision. texts.full_text: e enough support for me when my health declines, and what kind of life will that mean? These are valid concerns as we navigate an aging population. However, rest assured—it is possible to age gracefully with thoughtful planning. Firstly, it's essential to plan financially by consulting retirement experts or financial advisors who can help you secure your future while preparing for potential healthcare costs in old age. Secondly, regarding care: consider exploring long-term-care options early on and have open conversations about end-of-life wishes. This ensures that both the individual’s needs are met as well as their family's preparedness to handle such responsibilities if needed. R

texts.full_text: vere dermatitis have increased risk factors not only developing respiratory allergy but also rhinitis and allergic conjunctivitis . The most common concomitant diseases observed in children affected by atopic eczema were: bronchial hyperreactivity, food allergies , Allergic rhinoconjunctivis. The combination of asthma/rhinits is known as “allergy triad”, the association between AD with Asthma or Rhino-sinusitis has been named "atopic tetrad". The relationship among these diseases seems to be particularly evident in children . A strong link exists even at a genetic level, several genes associated and implicated both on skin barrier dysfunction (FLG/SPINK5) , immune dysregulation of Th2 response(IL13 variant rs64907 vs. IL-8 haplotypes ) have been identified as risk factors for AD development in children . The most common concomitant diseases observed are: bronchial hyperreactivity, food allergies and allergic rhinoconjunctivitis; the combination between asthma/rhinits is known “allergy triad”.

texts.full_text: findings.[2](#jah31252-bib-0002){ref-type="ref"} The 2013American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) HF guidelines recommend that patients with HF be classified according to the following 4 categories: HF with reduced EF (HFrEF), HF with preserved EF (HFpEF), HF with midrange EF (HFmEF), and HF with unknown EF (HFUEF).[2](#jah31252-bib-0002){ref-type="ref"} Although this classification system is a useful tool to aid in the clinical management of patients with HF, it is important to recognize that the ACC/AHA classification scheme does not provide information about the underlying pathophysiology of the HF syndrome. Hence, this classification scheme does not take into account the specific cause of HF in each patient. This is important because the cause of HF can vary by patient and can be due to structural heart disease, valvular heart disease, cardiomyopathy, or myocarditis. Furthermore, the prevalence of ADHF and its mortality rate vary based on the underlying etiology of HF.[3](#jah31252-bib-0003){ref-type="ref"} The purpose of our study was to describe the clinical characteristics and outcomes of patients presenting with ADHF in a large, community-based hospital system. Methods {#jah31252-sec-0005} ======= Study Design and Patient Selection ---------------------------------- We conducted a retrospective cohort study of consecutive patients admitted to all 3 hospitals affiliated with the University of California, San Francisco

For each $c \in \{2, \ldots, c_0\}$, let $\hat{C}^{(c)}$ be the set of users that are accused because their scores $S_j^{(c)}$ exceeded $Z^{(c)}$ before $t^{(c)} > \ell^{(c)}$. Then $\hat{C} = \bigcup_{c=2}^{c_0} \hat{C}^{(c)}$. For any $c$, we can apply Theorem \[thm:PDS-DT-Soundness\] to the parameters $d_{\ell}^{(c)}, d_z^{(c)}, d_{\delta}^{(c)}, a^{(c)}$ and ${\epsilon}_1^{(c)}$ so that we know that the probability that $j \in \hat{C}^{(c)}$ for innocent users $j$ is at most ${\epsilon}_1^{(c)}/n$. So the overall probability that an innocent user is disconnected is bounded from above by $$\begin{aligned} {P}(j \in \hat{C}) \leq \sum_{c=2}^{c_0} {P}(j \in \hat{C}^{(c)}) \leq \sum_{c=2}^{c_0} \frac{{\epsilon}_1^{(c)}}{n} \leq \frac{{\epsilon}_1}{n}.\end{aligned}$$ This completes the proof. Note that one can choose values ${\epsilon}_1^{(c)}$ satisfying such that $O(c^2 \ln(n/{\epsilon}_1^{(c)})) = O(c^2 \ln(n/{\epsilon}_1))$, e.g., by taking ${\epsilon}_1^{(c)} = 6{\epsilon}_1/(\pi^2 c^2)$. If furthermore $c = n^{o(1)}$ is subpolynomial in $n$, then asymptotically $d_{\ell} c^2 \ln(n/{\epsilon}_1^{(c)}) = d_{\ell} c^2 \ln(n/{\epsilon}_1) (1 + o(1))$ and we achieve the same asymptotic codelength as in the static and dynamic Tardos schemes. texts.full_text: the use of the data held in the register file 408, a plurality of timing registers 406, 407, 412, 413, 415, 416, 417, 419 and 424 for holding the parallel processed data in the respective stages into which the process of the machine instruction is divided. The microprocessor further comprises an instruction cache 401, an instruction aligner 402, a program counter 403, an incremental counter 404, an address selector 405, bypass selectors 409 and 410, arithmetic output selectors 418 and 423, a data address register 420, a data cache 421, a data aligner 422 and a write buffer 425. The microprocessor of the first example divides the process of a machine instruction into the following five stages; IF (Instruction Fetch), RF (Register Fetch), EX (Execute), DF (Data Fetch) and WB (Write Back), and parallely processes the movement as follows in the respective stages. In the stage of IF, the microprocessor reads a machine instruction from the instruction cache 401 through the instruction aligner 402, and holds the data in the timing register 406. In the stage of RF, the microprocessor decodes the machine instruction read out therefrom, decides the type of the operation, reads out the data within the register operand-designated by the instruction from the register file 408, and holds the data in the timing registers 412 and 413, or the timing registers from 415 to 417. In the stage of EX, the microprocessor executes the operation decided by the machine instruction by the use of the read data within the register, and holds the result in the timing register 419 or writes the operation result into the program counter 403 as an address, thereby performing an instruction branch. It has been used by television broadcasters to enable both 4:3 and 16:9 television sets to optimally present pictures transmitted in either format. It has also been used by broadcasters to dynamically control how down-conversion equipment formats widescreen 16:9 pictures for 4:3 displays. Standard AFD codes provide information to video devices about where in the coded picture the active video is and also the "protected area" which is the area that needs to be shown. If you want to associate two unit tests as part of the same test run in their respective .cs files (test1.cs and test2.Cs), both methods should return the exact same "guid" value. Here's a simple example how your method might look like: ```csharp private string GetCurrentRunGuid() { // retrieve TFS build guid here, it could be obtained from various sources e.g. system environment variables or build task inputs. return Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("MSBuild_Project_Tree_guid"); } ``` In this example the `GetCurrentRunGuid()` method retrieves a common shared unique identifier (guid) for your TFS build which both tests would then capture and use within their test methods. Remember, it's important that all unit tests in any given build share same "guid"

texts.full_text: nuclear deal from the Diplomatic Reception Room of the White House in Washington, May 8, 2018. Bolton argued that the nuclear deal, formally known as the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA), provided Iran with resources that it used to fund military activities across the region. "If you look at the advances that Iran has made under cover of this agreement, its conventional military and terrorist advances, in Iraq, in Syria, in Lebanon, in Yemen, since 2015, Iran was really on the march," Bolton said. "They were shifting the balance of power in the Middle East, until President Trump got out of this deal." Bolton also addressed the upcoming move of the U.S. embassy in Israel from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem, a decision that has sparked controversy and protests. He defended the move, saying it was a recognition of reality. "Recognizing reality always enhances the chances for peace," Bolton said. "It was a quid pro quo for the Israelis to say, 'We'll recognize West Jerusalem as the capital of Israel, and we'll continue to negotiate the borders of the state, the borders of sovereignty, and we'll see what we can do to make it better.'" Bolton's comments come as the Trump administration prepares to officially move the U.S. embassy to Jerusalem on May 14, 2018, a move that has been praised by Israel but has drawn criticism from Palestinian leaders and some international allies. In the broader context, the Trump administration's foreign policy has been marked by a series of bold and sometimes controversial decisions, including the withdrawal from the Iran nuclear deal and the recognition of Jerusalem as Israel's capital. texts.full_text: in the active medium like in the picture, what carriers are we talking about? Electrons? Holes? -Where are the levels of the laser here? And in what direction is the light emitted? A: The cladding used here is a semiconductor right? In practice, yes. In principle, though, it could be any material that has its primary function to confine carriers and guide optical modes within an active region of different optical index than itself. - When we talk about "confining the carriers" in quantum-confined lasers such as Quantum Well (QW) or Semiconductor Laser Diodes, what is usually used are both electrons and holes. In a semiconductor laser structure: - electrons originate from donor particles introduced into one of the layers. - Holes, on the other hand, come about due to acceptor doping in the layer. In structures like quantum wells or dots embedded within cladding materials (usually higher bandgap semiconductors), these carriers are confined spatially. This confinement leads them predominantly into discrete energy levels rather than a continuous distribution of energies as they could have been present without such constraints, essentially leading to enhanced recombination efficiency and optical gain at different frequencies.

texts.full_text: parated calendar picker is used to update the data table (Anything after the selected date will be displayed). The calendar picker works fine if no column filter has been used. However, the calendar picker stop to update the data table if one of the column filters is used. There is no error found either in the log or though the debugger. This issue seems related to how PrimeFaces handles multiple updates and filtering events on a single page when using `p:ajax` listeners for both components (the calendar picker and DataTable filter). ### Possible Causes: 1. Event Interference: When you apply filters, it might be interfering with the update event triggered by selecting dates from your Calendar. 2. Partial Page Updates Conflicts: If multiple partial updates are being processed simultaneously or in quick succession without proper handling of their lifecycle phases. ### Solutions: #### 1. Use `process` and/or `update` Ensure that you're specifying which components to process (`<p:ajax ... update="...">`) when an event is triggered by either the calendar picker or filter input fields: ```xml <p:dataTable id="dataTable" var="item" value="#{yourBean.items}" widgetVar="dtWidget"> <f:facet name="header"> Calendar Picker and Filters Demo. </f:facet> <!-- Columns with filters --> ... </p:dataTable> <!-- Separate calendar picker to update the data table. Ensure it processes only components or even all if needed for your case --> <p:datePicker id="calendar" value="#{yourBean.selectedDate}"> <p:ajax event="dateSelect" listener="#{yourBean.onDatePickerChange}" process="@this dataTableFiltersIdIfAnyOtherThanDataTableItselfAndCalendarHere!" update="dataTable"/> </p:datePicker> ``` #### 2. Use `resetValues` in `<f:param>` To ensure that the form values are reset when you select a date, which might help with conflicts between filters and updates: ```xml <p:ajax event="dateSelect" listener="#{yourBean.onDatePickerChange}" update="@form" process="@this"> <p/resetInput target="dataTable"/> </p:ajax> ``` #### 3.

texts.full_text: of the trial judge, in ordering this personal property returned to McCrary, that exception was taken anappeal was perfected by this State as plaintiff-in-error. The sole question presented is whether or not the court erred when he ordered possession of these items restored to defendant upon a motion made subsequent to the jury's verdict acquitting McCrary, who had been indicted for possessing a male deer without antlers in violation of Section 51-425, T.C.A.. Section 38-709 of Tennessee Code Annotated provides: "All property seized under this part shall be retained by the state until final judgment and disposition of any prosecution therefor; but if no indictment is found or information filed within five (5) months from date of seizure, said property may be returned to its owner upon application." This statute clearly gives the trial court jurisdiction over seized property pending the outcome of a case. However, once the defendant has been acquitted by a jury, it could reasonably be argued that there is no longer any basis for retaining his property under this section. In support of its position, plaintiff cites State v. Carroll (1958), where we held that when an indictment is quashed or dismissed before trial begins, the seizure and detention are illegal as there's no pending case to justify them; hence, seized property should be restored immediately without waiting for final judgment. But here, unlike in State v. Carroll, McCrary was tried by a jury who found him not guilty – thereby acquitting him of all charges – which makes this case distinguishable from that precedent since the trial has concluded with an outcome favoring the defendant. Given these circumstances and considering that no other proceedings related to this seizure are pending nor have been initiated against McCrary, we find merit in his argument for restoration of possession following acquittal at trial. Thus, based on our review, there was neither abuse or error committed by the lower
